title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Universal Basic Income (UBI)

texts.A: The proposal for a Universal Basic Income is often dismissed as a utopian fantasy, yet it is a pragmatic response to the looming automation of the workforce. By decoupling survival from traditional employment, we allow individuals the freedom to pursue education, caregiving, or entrepreneurial ventures without the specter of destitution. Critics argue it would lead to mass idleness, but pilot programs suggest the opposite: people use the floor of financial security to leap into more meaningful work. It is not a handout; it is a foundational right in a post-industrial economy.
texts.B: Implementing UBI would be an economic catastrophe of unprecedented proportions. The sheer cost of providing a meaningful monthly payment to every citizen would necessitate tax hikes that would stifle investment and drive talent abroad. Furthermore, the psychological importance of 'work' cannot be overstated; it provides structure, purpose, and social integration. Replacing the dignity of earned income with a state stipend risks creating a permanent underclass characterized by apathy and a loss of agency. We should focus on wage subsidies and retraining, not unconditional cash.
texts.C: While I share the skepticism regarding the fiscal viability of a full-scale UBI, we cannot ignore that our current welfare systems are paternalistic and inefficient. A 'negative income tax' or a simplified grant system would eliminate the bureaucratic hurdles that currently trap people in poverty. However, the idea that UBI alone will solve the automation crisis is naive. Without parallel investments in public housing and healthcare, any cash injection will simply be absorbed by rising rents and private service costs. The focus must be on 'Universal Basic Services' rather than just liquid cash.
texts.D: The debate over UBI is essentially a debate over the nature of human motivation. Those who believe people only work under the threat of starvation have a very dim view of our species. I agree that UBI provides the essential 'exit option' that allows workers to refuse exploitative conditions, thereby forcing the labor market to improve. Unlike those who fear an economic meltdown, I believe the increased local spending power would trigger a grassroots economic renaissance. The real challenge is not 'can we afford it,' but 'can we afford the social cost of not doing it' as inequality reaches breaking points.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the psychological impact of receiving an unconditional income?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes UBI risks creating 'apathy' and a loss of 'dignity.' Expert A argues that it actually empowers people to pursue 'more meaningful work' and entrepreneurial ventures.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's opinion about the effect of UBI on the power dynamics of the labor market?
questions.explanation: Expert D says UBI allows workers to 'refuse exploitative conditions.' Expert A similarly describes it as giving individuals 'freedom' and an alternative to 'traditional employment.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the limitations of UBI as a standalone solution?
questions.explanation: Expert C argues that UBI won't work without other investments (housing, etc.). Expert B also argues for alternatives like 'wage subsidies and retraining,' agreeing that simple cash isn't the silver bullet.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the financial consequences of implementing UBI?
questions.explanation: Expert D believes UBI would trigger an 'economic renaissance.' Expert B, however, predicts an 'economic catastrophe' and 'stifled investment.'
